entry.title: Re: Automatic conversion of quantities-unit of measure on...
entry.content:
Hi,

 

If you want to display one of the stock line with BUoM and Alt. UoM for physical stock, Please go to APP Warehouse Monitor -> Physical Stock, Select the stock line -> Click More method -> Change Alternative UoM.

 

But if you want the stock to be putaway into the bin with BAG as Alt.UoM automatically when the stock is moved into the bin. Please follow below steps:

  1. Move all the stock out of the storage type and leave one stock entry, convert the Alt.UoM as BAG.
  2. Stet the "QuantAddnStk AUoM" set as "First Alternative UoM Dominant" and "Level of Avail. Qty" can be set as "Storage Bin" at Customizing "Maintain Storage Types".
  3. Move the rest of the stock back.

KBA 3616708 Customizing "Maintain Storage Types"-> "QuantAddnStk AUoM" set as "First Alternative UoM Dominant" doesn't work - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

 

Best Regards,

entry.title: Handler for Button not called in adaptation
entry.content:
Hello everybody,

I try to extend a standard App "Create maintenance request" (F1511A). I have added a Fragment with button and the button is available:

My Fragment look like this:

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns:core='sap.ui.core' xmlns='sap.m'>
    <Button id='LorCapBtn' text="LorCap" press='extension.customer.create.notif.variant.onLorCapPress'/> 
</core:FragmentDefinition>

The controller:

sap.ui.define(
    [
        'sap/ui/core/mvc/ControllerExtension'
        ,'sap/m/MessageBox'
        // ,'sap/ui/core/mvc/OverrideExecution'
    ],
    function (
        ControllerExtension
        ,MessageBox
        // ,OverrideExecution
    ) {
        'use strict';
        return ControllerExtension.extend("customer.create.notif.variant.MainCntrlExt", {
            onLorCapPress : function(oEvent){
                console.log("onLorCapPress called");
                MessageBox.success("Hello");
            },
});
    }
);

When I press the button nothing happened (no massege box, no message in console).

Do you know what is the problem? What have to be done to run my code?

Regards,

Boguslaw

entry.title: Re: 生产订单联产品结算规则必填
entry.content:
你好，在生产订单中，结算规则为必填项，且无法为空，但可以通过调整结算规则的方法来支持这种特殊情况下的成本分摊。例如，使用特定的成本分配方法来处理未产出的联产品成本。

  1. 利用不同的结算规则和等值数：

    • 使用特定的结算规则（如PP5或PP6）分配生产成本，这些规则允许用计划产出数量或实际交货数量来设置等值数，从而适应生产过程中联产品未产出的情况，参考链接：联产品制造中的结算规则 | SAP Help Portal

  2. 使用事件驱动的生产会计：

    • 在事件驱动的生产会计中，可以为非固定价格的联产品使用分摊法进行成本分配，该方法基于订单头维护的分摊规则，参考链接：基于事件的成本分配（联产品和副产品） | SAP Help Portal。

从业务场景和联产品的业务定义上看，若业务流程流转到制造执行过程中，生产因为临时工艺调整导致最终的产成品发生变化（如最终产成品不等同于生产订单抬头物料）。理论上应该关闭当前生产订单，建立新的生产订单。

其次，如果此类情况经常发生，建议客户可以从业务重要性重新评估是否需要将该产品定义为联产品。并且重新考虑该产品对于主产品的成本影响和占比，以及是否需要将其改定义为副产品。

建议联系您的咨询服务商进行业务流程背景的深入探讨寻求更为适合的解决方案。

以上信息供参考。

谢谢

entry.title: Q2 2025: Quarterly Release Highlights: SAP BTP Security and Identity & Access Management
entry.content:
In the second quarter of 2025, we released several new features and enhancements. Did you know that you can find all new feature announcements for SAP Cloud Identity Services in the SAP Cloud Identity Services Release Notes on the SAP Help Portal?

Let’s take a look at some of our recent highlights!

Identity Provisioning Service: Job log improvements

The Job Execution Outcome is now displayed on the Job Execution Details screen and included in the job notifications. It appears when delta-read jobs and jobs that fail to read entities from the source system affect the deletion of entities in the target system.
For more information, see Monitor Provisioning Job Logs.

You can now download and view the details of all deleted entities by a provisioning job. To enable this, set the property ips.trace.deleted.entity to true.
For more information, see Manage Provisioning Job Logs and List of Properties.

Identity Directory Service and Identity Provisioning Service: Support for extension schemas towards many SAP business applications

We now support a number of extension schemas, including

  • SAP extension schema for groups
  • Extension enterprise schema for users
  • SAP ODM User Schema Support

You can find details in the SAP Cloud Identity Release Notes.

Extension of the Identity Directory’s SCIM API

The Identity Directory API of SAP Cloud Identity Services now returns the termsOfUse and privacyPolicy parameters. For more information, check Identity Directory API.

In addition, we now support application-specific group assignments. You can find details in the SAP Help Portal chapters on Identity Authentication and the Local Identity Directory.

Management of Corporate Identity Providers via API

Tenant administrators can manage corporate identity providers in Identity Authentication via API. It offers endpoints for CRUD operations (GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE) over the corporate identity providers. See SAP Cloud Identity Services Corporate Identity Providers Directory.

Configuration of Password Expiration Reminder

You can configure the system to show a password expiration reminder on the user’s sign-in page. For more information, check Enable Password Expiration Reminder.

Enabling SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI with support for Microsoft Windows mandatory profiles

We added support for single sign-on on shopfloor Windows-based computers that are configured to use a mandatory profile. This protects shopfloor computers with a restrictive mandatory profile while still offering single sign-on support for desktop clients of SAP GUI. You can find more information in the SAP Roadmap Explorer.

Cloud Foundry Login Based on JSON Web Tokens (JWTs)

When building CI/CD pipelines, developers are responsible for managing the credentials used by these pipelines for authentication to external systems. This traditionally requires developers to take care of the lifecycle of the credentials, including secure storage and regular rotation of the credentials, which comes with repeated efforts and might be error-prone.

In his latest blog post, Authenticating GitHub Actions Workflows Deploying to the SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Environment with JWTs, Sebastian Ries explains how to authenticate to the SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry environment from a GitHub Actions workflow using GitHub-issued short-lived JSON Web Tokens (JWTs), eliminating the need for manual secret management and enabling secure, efficient CI/CD workflows. 

Want to stay up to date on our services? Join our SAP BTP Security, Single Sign-On for SAP GUI and SAP Cloud Identity Services communities!

entry.title: Re: Regarding CAP Java target and custom error messages
entry.content:
There are specific annotations for input validations that create specific error messages: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/providing-services#input-validation However, there are no annotations to define custom error messages for a field.

It wouldn't be enough to specify the content of the error message, but you'd also need to specify when to throw these. You have to do this programmatically in an event handler and use the message target API to indicate the elements targeted by your error.

See https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/java/event-handlers/indicating-errors#target for more details.

entry.title: Embracing AI in Field Service: From Garden Tools to Smart Assistance
entry.content:
(Blog 2 of the “Transformation in Field Service” series)

When we first added a smart irrigation system to our garden, I was skeptical. Could a sensor and some automation really replace the watchful care of a gardener? It didn’t. But it did make us smarter. We still prune, plant, and pick. But now we get alerts before things dry out, insights about soil health, and more time for what really matters: growing beautiful things.

That, to me, is what AI should feel like in Field Service.

Why AI Is Top of Mind

AI is no longer a futuristic buzzword. With the rise of Generative AI and machine learning, it's becoming a real part of how organizations operate. For Field Service companies, transformation is top of mind—and not without reason.

Service teams face serious pressure:

  • Customers expect faster response and personalized service

  • Skilled labor is increasingly scarce

  • Costs are rising, while expectations keep growing

AI offers a way to cope. But here’s the catch: it only helps if it fits the people who need to use it.

Field Service: A Surprising AI Frontrunner?

You might assume AI will take hold first in data-driven industries like finance or marketing. But Field Service might just beat them to it.

Why? Because AI in Field Service solves exactly the things many technicians don’t want to deal with. These are hands-on experts who often chose their profession precisely to avoid sitting in front of software.

If AI can:

  • Remove repetitive tasks

  • Reduce time spent on documentation

  • Automate job filtering and planning

  • Provide guidance without asking questions

…then it becomes not just useful—but welcome. And that’s where adoption begins.

AI Doesn’t Replace People—It Supports Them

AI in Field Service doesn’t replace know-how, experience, or context. It supports the humans doing the work—giving them tools to focus on what they do best.

  • Dispatchers get intelligent support to make faster, better-informed scheduling decisions

  • Technicians get equipment insights, task summaries, and job predictions—without needing to dig through software

  • Managers gain better visibility into performance and planning scenarios

It’s not about handing over control—it’s about working smarter.

What’s Already Available in SAP Field Service Management

SAP Field Service Management already includes a growing suite of AI features designed to make life easier for dispatchers and technicians. Here are a few highlights:

  • Joule for Dispatchers (Doc, Video)
    Boost efficiency and get quick access to help documentation with a natural language assistant.

  • Intelligent Filtering (Doc, Video)
    Use natural language to search and filter jobs more intuitively and accurately.

  • Equipment Insights (Doc, Video)
    Get smart summaries of past jobs and proactive equipment monitoring.

  • Activity Summary (Doc, Video)
    See how similar issues were resolved—improving repair planning and speed.

  • Predictive Routing (Doc, Video)
    Minimize travel time by finding the most efficient route.

  • Job Duration Prediction (Doc, Video)
    Estimate how long a job will take using Machine Learning.

  • AI Policy Designer & Auto-scheduling (Doc, Video)
    Set up rules and simulate auto-scheduling tailored to your business logic.

  • Planning Simulations (Doc, Video)
    Visualize the impact of scheduling choices before you commit.

Each of these is designed not to replace humans—but to take work off their plate.

Embracing AI Thoughtfully

If you want your AI initiatives to succeed, treat them like a new team member:

  • Explain what it does—and what it doesn’t

  • Make its logic transparent and explainable

  • Start small, get feedback, and improve

  • Bring people along for the journey

Trust and adoption go hand in hand. Don’t expect people to embrace what they don’t understand.

Back to the Garden

AI won’t plant your seeds. It won’t know which flowers matter most to you. But it will remind you to water them, help you spot what’s missing, and give you more time to enjoy what you’ve grown.

The same is true in Field Service. Embracing AI isn’t about replacing people. It’s about helping them flourish—on their own terms.

Intro to the Series:

👉The Heart of Transformation: People, Process, and Persistence

SAP Field Service Management

Hi Team,

Error Details ::

422 Unprocessable Entity on POST request for "https://x-rn-srv/message": "{"errorDetails":{"code":"422 UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY","message":"Configuration error occurred: Bad Request","target":"/message","details":[]}}".


2) Here i"m integrating with SAP ALM to BTP IS.

we i"m facing with connection issue, partner is not assigned to SAPALM service. I tried to configure the service from SAPALM partner is not visible.

Need to setup the connection between SAPALM and BTP IS..

details ::

Log on to SAP BTP Cockpit and navigate to the sub-account for your SAP Build Process Automation 

  1. Go to Connectivity > Destinations
  2. Click on "New Destination"
  3. Create a new destination using the "Blank Template" view
    1. Name: Enter a name for the destination as sap_process_automation_cloud_alm
    2. Type: HTTP
    3. URL: SAP Cloud ALM service key parameter "Endpoints" > "Api" without api (make sure to keep the / at the end)
    4. Authentication: Select 'OAuth2ClientCredentials'
    5. Client ID: SAP Cloud ALM service key parameter "clientid"
    6. Client Secret: SAP Cloud ALM service key parameter "clientsecret"
    7. Token Service URL: SAP Cloud ALM service key parameter "uaa" > "url" + /oauth/token


Required connection setup URL's to specify..

entry.title: Understanding Identity and Access Management (IAM) in your SAP Landscape
entry.content:
As companies adopt cloud services, hybrid infrastructures, and mobile workforces, traditional security models no longer hold up. That’s where Identity and Access Management (IAM) steps in as a foundational element of modern cybersecurity strategies.

What is IAM?

IAM, or Identity and Access Management, refers to the set of processes, technologies, and policies that ensure the right individuals—employees, partners, customers—have access to the right resources at the right times for the right reasons. It encompasses how users are authenticated, how their access is granted or revoked, and how their digital identities are managed throughout their lifecycle.

In short, IAM is about answering two key questions:

  • Who are you?
  • What are you allowed to do in the system landscape?

Properly implemented IAM safeguards critical systems and data from unauthorized access, minimizes risk, and supports regulatory compliance. It’s a key enabler for digital transformation!

IAM in the Context of the Secure Operations Map (SOM)

In SAP’s Secure Operations Map (SOM), IAM falls under the User & Identity Management domain. This domain addresses how user identities are created, maintained, and authenticated across enterprise systems. It’s closely tied to both operational efficiency and the ability to secure access in a consistent, auditable way.

This positioning reflects IAM’s importance not just as a security function, but as a business enabler. IAM is a central element for maintaining secure, scalable operations across SAP and non-SAP landscapes.

IAM in the Context of the Secure Operations Map (SOM)

Why is IAM So Crucial Today?

IAM has evolved in response to the shifting landscape of IT security. To understand its current importance, it helps to contrast legacy models with today’s Zero Trust approach.

Legacy Security Model: Perimeter-Based Thinking

Traditionally, enterprise security was focused on protecting the network perimeter. Tools like firewalls, intrusion detection systems, and VPNs were used to keep threats out. The assumption was that anything inside the network could be trusted.

However, this model has several flaws:

  • Insider threats remain unmitigated.
  • It doesn’t scale well to cloud or remote access.
  • Once attackers breach the perimeter, they can move laterally with little resistance.

Zero Trust Security Model: Trust Nothing by Default

In contrast, Zero Trust assumes breach as a baseline. It operates on the principle of “never trust, always verify.” In this model:

  • Every user and device must authenticate continuously.
  • Access is granted based on identity, context, and least privilege.
  • Identity becomes the new security perimeter.

This is why modern IAM is more than just a directory or a login page—it's the core of Zero Trust architecture.

Identity becomes the new security perimeter

SAP Cloud Identity Services (SCI): Your IAM Gateway

Within the SAP ecosystem, the primary tool for IAM is SAP Cloud Identity Services (SCI). SCI is not just a technical necessity—it’s a strategic enabler that helps organizations innovate faster and more securely.

What is SAP Cloud Identity Services?

SAP Cloud Identity Services is a suite of cloud-based solutions designed to provide seamless, secure, and scalable identity management across SAP and third-party applications. Its key components are:

  • Identity Authentication Service (IAS): Handles user login and Single Sign-On (SSO), with support for modern authentication protocols like SAML, OAuth, and OpenID Connect.
  • Identity Provisioning Service (IPS): Automates the creation, update, and removal of user accounts across connected systems. Reduces manual effort and risk of human error.
  • Identity Directory: Stores user attributes and acts as a central repository for identity data, enabling fine-grained control over access.
  • Authorization Management: Helps to define and manage fine-grained access policies for SAP BTP applications based on user or business object attributes. Assign policies using group management in the identity directory, either via SAP Cloud Identity Services UI or the SCIM API.

Together, these services allow organizations to unify identity management across cloud, hybrid, and on-premise systems—accelerating time-to-value while improving compliance and security.

SAP IAM integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services

How to Get Started with IAM in SAP?

If you're new to IAM or just beginning your journey with SAP Cloud Identity Services, here’s a roadmap to guide you:

  • Explore the IAM Reference Architectures: Understand the big picture by reviewing SAP’s recommended architectures for IAM: IAM Reference Architectures

  • Get Hands-On with Tutorials: Try out SCI in a test environment to see how provisioning works in practice. You can also start with a mission on SAP discovery center https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/3116/3152/

  • Collaborate Across Teams: IAM is not a siloed effort. Talk to your Identity & Access Management colleagues and align on strategies for integrating supported systems. Supported Systems in SCI

  • Involve Experts Early: IAM often reaches beyond the SAP center of competence into enterprise architecture, HR systems, compliance, and even customer experience. Bring in experts who can help you design processes that are secure, user-friendly, and scalable.

Final Thoughts

Implementing IAM is not just about ticking a compliance box or improving login flows. It’s about protecting your organization’s most valuable assets—its data, systems, and people—while enabling agility and innovation.

IAM is a journey, not a one-time project. As you build your foundation with SAP Cloud Identity Services, keep in mind that success depends on both technical integration and cross-functional collaboration.

The important IAM domain of secure authentication will be covered in my next post, where I’ll  share practical tips on how to ensure secure connection works both in cloud and on premises.

entry.title: Re: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Composite Roles in launchpad content and layout tools
entry.content:
Hello @K4I,

is the space part of the role menu of the composite role? If yes, could you try to delete it from there and then create a single role only containing the space and adding this single role to the already existing composite role? This is the way Jocelyn describes in the "Exemplary composite role structure with shared space..." illustration. Does it work then? You could also check after this, if it still works if you derive the "space-only" role and add this instead of the master "space-only" role to the composite role.

If not, maybe the following SAP note will help, describing an issue with derived roles not being fully supported in the Manage Launchpad Pages application: 3625412 - Error "Out of role context" when editing launchpad pages with composite roles in SAP Fiori Launchpad

Best regards
Simon

entry.title: Re: how to transition SAC on BW/4HANA to SAC on BDC
entry.content:
@Supatchj23 

There are couple of ways to migrate BW to BDC and they depend on how deep is your current investment in BW. If you want to preserve your existing BW investment you could opt for migrating BW to PCE and treat it as a data product. The other option is to rebuild your DW layer fresh using Datasphere artifacts.

entry.title: Compensation Information - Delimit Allowance record
entry.content:
Hi All, 

We encountered a scenario where the Compensation Planner removed an allowance in the compensation worksheet by setting the manual amount to "0". As a result, the final amount becomes "0", and when the record is published to EC, the allowance amount appears as "0".

However, we noticed that amounts with "0" are not being replicated into ECP. This leads to an issue during payroll processing, as the allowance record remains active in ECP without a delimitation, and the employee still receives the allowance.

Example:

  • 26/06/2024 – Employee #123 has:

  • Basic Salary: 1500
  • Car Allowance: 450

  • Effective 26/06/2025 – Employee #123 should have:

  • Basic Salary: 2000
  • Skills Allowance: 500
  • Car Allowance: removed (set to 0 in worksheet)

In the compensation worksheet, the planner entered:

  • 0 for Car Allowance
  • 500 for Skills Allowance

After publishing to EC, the record for 26/06/2025 shows:

  • Car Allowance: 0
  • Basic Salary: 2000
  • Skills Allowance: 500

However, during replication to ECP, the Car Allowance is not updated/delimited, resulting in the employee still receiving it in payroll.

How should this scenario be handled?
Is there a way to ensure that the Car Allowance record is delimited properly so it is not paid in ECP?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: SAP B1 BY-PRODUCT
entry.content:
Hi @mari_outtaleb96 ,

I'm a bit confused. Your statement: Explanation: Item costs = item BOM cost + sub-product cost. Is this the current outcome or the desired one? As you further down say: My goal is to have the pure item cost without the cost of the sub-product.

As per your screenshots, I assume the By-Product is "DECHET-HGT", but it is not entered as negative, which is how SAP recognises a By-Product. As you did not provide screenshots from the journal entry, I can only assume that DECHET-HGT is being added to the cost of SARDINE-HGT, which sounds normal, as positive quantities do add cost to the final product.

Normally, with your By-Product item as negative, and assigned to a different warehouse, it should not impact the cost of the finished goods. Please give it a try and share the results.

BR,
Felipe

entry.title: My Inbox App customizing
entry.content:
Hi all, 

I wonder if it is possible to customize the My Inbox App so that the "Texts" available in the Credit Memo Request Form could be displayed in My Inbox 

For Purchase Orders the fields "Notes" are displayed in My Inbox, which is very usefull for the approver who are able to read comments are internal notes related to the order. This can help them to make a decision about whether accepting or rejecting the order (see attachement "my_inbow_purchase_order_notes") 

But, despite similar fields "Texts" exist in the Credit Memo Request form (see attachement "credit_memo_request_form"), they are not displayed in My Inbox (see attachement "my_inbow_credit_memo_request") 

 

 

Would someone know if it is possible to customize My Inbox App template – in a 2Sl s4 hana public cloud system – so that the "Texts" filds could be displayed in the credit memo request approval view ?

Or if there  is an "easy way" to create a custom field in the Credit Memo Request Template and display it in My Inbox ? 

Thank you for your help, 

Erwan

entry.title: Understanding the impact of onboarding on the Assignment ID rule
entry.content:
In SuccessFactors Employee Central we have the Assignment ID field in Employment Info, by default this is populated during hire as the same value as the person id external.

If for example, we had a 9 digit code for person id external then the assignment ID would also be the same value.  If a third party systems needed an 8 digit code rather than 9 then you could utilise the employment setting configuration in Manage Data to create a rule as follows.  This rule gets triggered when you hire an employee and will set the Assignment ID to an 8 digit value.

In our example when we initiate onboarding the person id external is set with a 9 digit number and the assignment Id is also set as a 9 digit number.  The assignment rule is not triggered when you initiate onboarding, it is only triggered when the person is processed out of Manage Pending Hires.

Therefore, if we do not adjust the rule then the assignment ID will not be updated to an 8 digit number as the ‘if statement’ is looking for a ‘null’ value when it is actually already been set as a 9 digit code.

If you update the rule as follows this will set the Assignment ID to an 8 digit number.

Please note that until 2025H2 the assignment rule will not trigger for employees coming from onboarding as mentioned above, this is being fixed in the release.  In the meantime, if the rule is needed to be triggered please raise a support incident and SAP support can run a script to ensure the rule works as described.

entry.title: Multiple Country Activation in CBC
entry.content:
We need to activate multiple countries and company codes in SAP S/4 HANA public cloud implementation.

In SAP note 3151804, SAP recommends activating only 5 countries at a time. However, with multiple company codes to set up for each country, our assumption was that we have to complete the primary finance settings and organisational structure set up before we can activate more countries. Then I came across a footnote in SAP learning material below:

We recommend activating no more than 5 countries at a time for system performance purposes. This means choosing the first 5 countries you want to activate and selecting the Complete Activity button, then going back to activate any additional countries and selecting the Complete Activity button again.

I will like to confirm that we do not have to complete organisational structure set up for each group of 5 countries before we activate the next set of countries. i.e.  can we activate all the countries before moving on to primary finance settings and organisational structure activities in CBC?

entry.title: Where finance and carbon unite
entry.content:
Where finance and carbon unite: A focus on the integration between Sustainability Footprint Management, Sustainability Data Exchange, SAP Green Ledger and SAP S/4HANA

Integration has always been at the heart of my story telling approach and hopefully comes across in my blogs. In this blog, I'll explore how this theme connects various solutions like Sustainability Footprint Management with its detailed product footprint calculations, its integration with our core SAP S/4HANA Suite, and our latest innovation, the 'Green Ledger,' which unites finance and carbon accounting.

I'll illustrate these points using an example: how everything fits together when we manufacture a windshield.

  • I shall begin with a high level overview of the setup in SAP S/4HANA; I shall focus on the future enhancements on this space (Universal Component Breakdown UCB) in a separate blog which allows for a more granular breakdown of the product, not only the breakdown of cost components but also a breakdown of quantities (carbon to start with).
  • Then I shall delve into the detailed product footprint calculation capabilities offered by Sustainability Footprint Management (SFM). As I explore the purchase of raw materials, we shall see how we can obtain the carbon footprint directly from suppliers through Sustainability Data Exchange (SDX) or derive them from Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) databases. This is where we have some gen AI capabilities which help you to map the product or product groups to emission factors thereby streamlining the process and improving accuracy. I shall then demonstrate how these values seamlessly integrate with SAP S/4HANA, enabling businesses to make more informed sourcing decisions by considering the carbon impact alongside traditional cost factors. This integration empowers organizations to optimize their supply chain while minimizing their environmental footprint. Finally, I shall also model the energy flows in the manufacturing plant to calculate a detailed product footprint for the manufactured material.
  • Subsequently, all material movements (goods receipts, goods issues) from SAP S/4HANA are then integrated with SAP Green Ledger. Here, financial dimensions are enriched with carbon information derived from Sustainability Footprint Management.

This is a schematic representation of the solution landscape for the blog:

Let's address this step by step: I have worked as a Group controller for an Automotive component supplier, so I'll begin with an example that I am familiar with: the manufacture of windshields.

 Please note that this is a highly simplified example with fictional values for the components and production processes.

1. Manufacturing set-up in SAP S/4HANA

The production of a windshield undergoes the following steps: First, raw glass is procured from a supplier and cut to the desired shape. The edges are then ground using a grinding machine, and holes are drilled. Following these initial steps, the glass is washed and dried before logos are printed onto its surface. To achieve the correct curvature, the glass undergoes heat treatment in a furnace. After this, the glass is washed and dried once again before it is assembled with a PVB sheet. The assembled piece is then processed through an autoclave, which functions similarly to a pressure cooker, to enhance the glass's transparency. Finally, the windshield goes through a thorough inspection process. Upon passing inspection, the pre-assembly of additional components such as mirror brackets or camera supports can start. This marks the first stage of the windshield production.

I have modeled this process in SAP S/4HANA as a semi-integrated line, utilizing blue boxes to represent "work centers" or production lines.

The focus of this blog is on our sustainability narrative, so I will not delve into the production setup or costing details. However, I would like to highlight an important innovation being developed by our Product Engineering teams called “Universal Component Breakdown”:

The breakdown of a product or a service into its origin in various dimensions across multiple production/service levels within a company.

This is based upon a functionality which we already have in Product Costing called the “cost component split” which comes in two flavours – the production cost component split and the primary cost component split. In addition to a new and improved cost breakdown, our teams are also working on a “quantity breakdown” wherein the Product Costing application is enhanced to determine the GHG emissions in the standard cost estimate and can be rolled up along the value chain, in the same way that standard costs are rolled up for a manufactured product in a multi-level cost estimate. This functionality is how we approach the carbon accounting from a “bottom-up” perspective.

The “Universal Component Breakdown (UCB)”, which is a future successor of the cost component split  forms the basis of carbon sub-ledgers, has “Universal Parallel Accounting (UPA)” as a pre-requisite(as per current planning, but subject to change) , and I shall address this topic in a separate blog post.

I have kept my example simple, focusing primarily on “carbon” and product carbon footprint calculation.

However, in real life, there are more aspects related to sustainability that should be considered. For instance, one must ask questions such as:

  • How am I handling the glass that falls off as I cut my windshield to shape? Typically, these would be recycled back into the Flat Glass Production process.
  • How am I managing water usage in my cleaning process? Recycled water could be used, and this is something that should be measured and reported on.

All these considerations are crucial for a comprehensive approach to sustainability.

2. Calculation of the Product Footprints

When it comes to understanding the total environmental impact of a product, we need to consider the environmental impact at each of the following stages:

  • Upstream processes
  • Transformation processes within our plant
  • Downstream processes

This structured approach ensures a comprehensive assessment of the product's overall environmental footprint.

The emissions included in the product carbon footprint calculation are:

  • Upstream processes:
    • Raw material purchase and pre-processing (Scope 3.1)
    • Inbound transport (Scope 3.4)
  • Processing in our production facility:
    • Direct Emissions from owned or controlled sources (Scope 1)
    • Indirect Emissions from purchased energy consumed (Scope 2)
    • Upstream emissions of purchased fuel, electricity, transmission and distribution losses (Scope 3.3)
    • Production Waste (Scope 3.5)
  • Downstream processes:
    • Transport to final customer if paid by the manufacturer (Scope 3.4)
    • Downstream transport if paid by the customer (Scope 3.9)
    • Processing of sold products (Scope 3.10)
    • Use of sold products (Scope 3.11)
    • End of Life treatment (Scope 3.12)

In this blog, I will limit the scope of the footprint calculation to a cradle-to-gate scenario and consider the emissions related to Scope 3.1, Scope 1 and Scope 2 for simplicity. This means my focus will be on the upstream processes and the transformation within our production unit, which will define the boundary conditions of this analysis.

 I highly recommend blogs written by Nico if you would like to deep dive into our solution. You will find many insightful articles in the following link: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management: Q2-24 Upd... - SAP Community

 Let's begin with the upstream process (Scope 3.1 emissions):

In my example, I am purchasing raw glass, mirror brackets, and camera supports to manufacture my windshield. These components could be sourced from various suppliers. Additionally, the Finance department is often responsible for considering sustainability metrics when procuring raw materials, with the aim of reducing the product's environmental footprint.

The footprint information of the components can be obtained in the following ways:

  • Suppliers can directly provide CO2e emissions data.
  • CO2e emissions data can be sourced from Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) databases.
  • Additionally, a custom dataset can be developed by considering multiple LCA values, which can then be used to determine the emission factors.

What is Sustainability Data Exchange (SDX)?

It is solution specifically designed to securely exchange standardized sustainability data, including product footprints, along the value chain. 

It is a part of the SAP Business Network and uses the carbon data interoperability standards established by the Partnership for Carbon Transparency (PACT) hosted by the World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD). The solution also draws on SAP’s experience as a founding member of Catena-X and Confinity-X, where the program set the first technical specifications for the standardized exchange of carbon emissions data, allowing different emissions and accounting technology solutions to connect to and effectively share sustainability information.

SAP offers this Business network to onboard and interact with the SAP ecosystem, however, if your suppliers have other certified solutions which follow the Catena-X and PACT standards, you can connect inter-operably with those solutions to get the emission data from the suppliers

a. Use case 1: Emission factors obtained directly from suppliers

 Sustainability Data Exchange (SDX) helps you to share product carbon footprint data with business partners across your business network based on interoperable and standardized data models. It enables exchange of Scope 3 product footprint information between connected partners via a request/respond feature.

Purchasing entity Perspective:

The purchasing entity can electronically request carbon footprint data from its suppliers via the SDX platform. On my homepage, there are dedicated apps that allow me to send requests to my suppliers (Manage Inbounds) and receive requests from my customers (Manage Outbounds), facilitating the exchange of carbon footprint information.

Please note that this blog does not cover the setup process. Instead, it focuses on the perspective of the business user who will be utilizing this solution. From the purchasing entity’s perspective, I can log on to SDX and send a “form-based request” to my supplier to provide the product footprint (in different standards: for example, WBSCD, PACT, CATENA-X). Once the request is sent, the status will indicate that a footprint has been requested from the supplier.

Supplier Side:

Suppliers can maintain detailed product footprints of the components they sell within SDX, as illustrated below. As mentioned earlier, if your suppliers use any other certified solutions which follow the CATENA-X and PACT protocols, we can also integrate with them.

They would receive requests to provide their carbon footprint data. For instance, my supplier ZPR_V01 has connected to SDX and can see that I have requested this footprint information from them.

They can then submit their response digitally based on the “response” feature.

After a supplier submits footprint data, it is integrated into SFM via an API and pending review for release. Once approved, the supplier's footprint can be used to calculate the product's footprint inventory. In my case, I have already procured Raw Glass 1 from two suppliers and can now view the aggregated footprint for the product. In addition, you can also accept and decline incoming data in case you are doubtful of the figures or you are not happy with the data sources.  

These footprints can then be integrated back into SAP S/4HANA where this can be used to drive informed decision-making by incorporating sustainability indicators in our sourcing process.

If you are wondering why there are differing CO2e rates for the supplier ZPR_V01 (where I maintained 7,165 kgCO2e/kg of the product in SFM, compared to 2,866 kgCO2e in the screenshot above), the differing CO2e rates for supplier ZPR_V01 can be attributed to a unit of measure (UoM) conversion. While the CO2e is maintained in kilograms (kg), the product's UoM is in pieces. Since purchasing is done in pieces, there's a conversion from pieces to kg based on the material master's UoM conversion factor. This explains why the CO2e rate is 7,165 kgCO2e/kg in SFM, compared to 2,866 kgCO2e in the above screenshot.

b. Use case 2: Emission factors obtained from LCA databases

If the supplier cannot provide us with emission factor data, we can source it from official Emission Factors or Lifecycle Assessment (LCA) databases. We readily integrate data from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), DEFRA, EMBER and have partnerships with organizations like Ecoinvent, Carbonminds who provide the data in the required format. Additionally, we can upload data from other LCA databases by mapping it to the necessary format

In my example, I created a custom package and uploaded it into Sustainability Footprint Management (SFM). SFM offers two upload options for creating a custom package:

  • Simple Template: This is a streamlined version designed for essential information, ideal for simple use cases or testing purposes.
  • LCA Template: This is a more detailed option, offering flexibility by organizing information across multiple tabs.

Once this is done, I can then use AI to automatically generate emission factor mappings for my products or product groups without footprint. This is a huge time saver given the volumetry of data we need to deal with.

The similarity score is determined by three key parameters: the similarity of the product's name or description to the emission factor description (both short and long versions), the product's commodity code, and the product's country code, which is derived based on where it is sourced from.  

Let's continue with the production processes in our plant and the calculation of Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions associated with this:

Step 1: Managing source data

The first step in calculating the carbon footprint of our windshields involves extracting relevant data from our core ERP system - both master and transactional, and integrating it into Sustainability Footprint Management (SFM), the solution which is used to calculate the product carbon footprint (PCF). Once a connection has been set up between the S4 and SFM, the “Manage Replications ” app can be used to import all relevant master data from the core S4 system to SFM.  You can select the data object you want to import, start or schedule the replication. Additionally, an import using data files or API is also available if you are working with a non-integrated system.

In my case, I verified the imported master data to ensure my materials, work centers and activities have been imported correctly

  • Master Data:
    • Products: The specific products for which I am calculating the product carbon footprint.
    • Resources: The different work centers used in production
    • Activities: Activities that need to be considered in the calculation of the product footprint. In this example, I am calculating emissions based on machine run time and set-up time.
    • Cost centers: After replicating the cost centers, you can assign each cost center to the appropriate production line. This will enable the SAP Green Ledger to accurately track and make sense of energy-related postings

For example, in the figure below, you can see the different work centers/production lines and their corresponding cost center assignments.

  • Transactional Data:

During the production process, the following data is recorded in the core S/4 system and subsequently   imported into SFM in a periodic and aggregated manner:

  • Resources used in the production process: Documented through Activity Declarations or Service Confirmations.
  • Component consumption: Tracked via Goods Issued to Production.
  • Finished goods declarations: Noted through Goods Receipt from Production.
  • Goods receipts from suppliers, Goods issues to customers, etc etc

Here is a screenshot illustrating how a 'Goods Issue for Production' import appears in SFM.

Here is a screenshot illustrating how an ‘Activity declaration’ or ‘internal service confirmation’ import appears in SFM

I will calculate the emissions from the consumption of electricity during the machine's operation and setup, as well as the emissions associated with the components consumed, to determine the total emissions for the finished goods assembly.

Step 2: Defining organizational boundaries

The subsequent step involves defining the scope and organizational boundaries for footprint calculation. At this stage, I've selected certain parts of the production value chain to be included in the footprint calculation. Currently, the following are factored in:

  • Purchased product footprint related to raw material acquisition (Scope 3.1)
  • Direct emissions and energy consumption used in production (Scope 1 and Scope 2)

To expand the footprint calculation in the future, I could incorporate:

  • Purchased and internal product transportation (Scope 3.4)
  • Emissions generated from “waste from operations” gathered via a forms-based data collection method.

Additionally, we can model any activity for which we aim to calculate Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 emissions as a “Business Activity.” We can then use a form-based data collection method to enter data and compute the corresponding emissions. One example of such an activity is business travel undertaken by project leaders working on a product prototype.

For instance, glass breakage could be recycled in the float process or sent to a landfill with corresponding emissions associated with this. The ability to refine the footprint calculation at a later stage gives customers flexibility to tailor the assessment to their specific needs and priorities, often starting small before getting to a full-fledged footprint calculation.

Step 3: Creating an Energy flow model

This step involves creating an energy flow model that maps energy consumption and distribution within your organization. This model connects energy carriers (Electricity, Fuel, Gas) with their respective sources (e.g., specific energy providers) and links them to resources (production lines/work centers) or facilities (plant, warehouse).

 Types of Energy used:

The various types of energy used are modeled as "Energy Carriers". In my example, there are two energy carriers:

  • Electricity
  • Gas

Energy Providers:

In my example, there are two energy providers:

  • EDF (Electricité de France) for Electricity
  • Engie for Gas

A contract between the legal entity and the utility (energy) provider (such as gas or electricity) is modeled as an "Energy Source" within SFM. EDF offers a choice between classical energy and renewable energy. Therefore, two separate contracts with EDF can be represented as individual "Energy Sources" in SFM. Similarly, Engie offers green gas as an option, which can also be modeled as an Energy Source in SFM.

Additionally, the cost center where energy invoices are posted before being allocated to production work centers can be assigned to these Energy Sources. This facilitates seamless integration with the General Ledger.

 Production Process: The production process is modeled as a semi-integrated line with the following key work centers:

  1. Furnace: This is an integrated line where cutting, grinding, drilling, washing, printing and heat treatment happen. Heat treatment being an energy-intensive process, this line has a dedicated electricity meter.
  2. Laminated Line: The PVB assembly is carried out in this line, which also has a separate electricity meter due to its high energy consumption.
  3. Quality and Pre-assembly: Quality control and pre-assembly of mirror brackets and camera supports happen here. These lines do not have dedicated meters and are covered by one common electricity meter.

Although my example focuses on the transformation of glass, it is important to note that the most energy-intensive process is not the transformation itself, but rather the production of the raw glass in the floats.

Energy Metering and Allocation:

There are four electricity meters:

  • One each for the "Furnace" and "Lamination" production lines, one for the “Quality” and “Pre-assembly” lines and finally one for the overall plant facility.
  • Energy consumption in the "Furnace" and "Lamination" lines is directly allocated to the products passing through these lines.
  • Energy consumption to the "Quality" and "Pre-assembly" production lines are split based on the relative machine run time and set up of these work centers.
  • Other Electricity used at the plant level does not go to the product carbon footprint, but rather to the corporate carbon footprint.

I have modeled the energy flows with meters, but there are multiple approaches to modeling energy flow in your plant, including:

Scenario 1: Energy Modeling Based on Planned Energy Consumption Rates

If your plant lacks energy meters for each production line, you can allocate energy bills based on the proportion of time each machine runs. Initially, you need to input the energy consumption rate per unit of time for each machine runs. The system then uses the actual activity declaration for each production order to calculate and distribute energy consumption accordingly.

Scenario 2: Energy Modeling Based on Meters

In this scenario, each resource (or production line/work center) has an associated energy meter. These meters provide energy consumption data, which can be linked to the number of hours a machine has run in producing a specific product. This data is then used to allocate CO2e for carbon footprint calculations across the production chain.

 Scenario 3: Energy Modeling Based on Meters and Planned Consumption Rates

In some cases, you might want to distribute energy consumption based on a combination of meters and planned consumption rates. This approach is useful when energy meters are only available for part of the organization.

Step 4 :  Creating a Footprint Inventory Scope

Upon importing the transactional data, energy meter readings, and any additional form-based information required, we shall then calculate a periodic footprint inventory for the entities within our organizational scope using the 'Calculate Footprint App

Once the footprint is calculated, you can drill down into the footprint results to see the detailed breakdown of all calculated footprint items, including products, suppliers, and more, as illustrated below:

Let's examine the footprint calculation in the first step of our production process:

In a real-world scenario, a windshield would have a more significant carbon footprint; however, the example provided here is a fictional and simplified one. Nonetheless, as expected, raw glass makes the largest contribution to the overall carbon footprint. The windshield's composition involves two different glass thicknesses, leading to variations in individual CO2E contributions. The energy consumption from the furnace is allocated to this production order. Within the production cost center, there are three activities (or resources): machine hours, setup time, and labor. The allocation of energy is based solely on machine hours and setup time, excluding labor. This explains why two of the activities have associated CO2E values, while the third does not.

In addition to the Sankey diagram, which visually breaks down emissions by components and processes, we are also introducing a new feature: a 'flat itemization.' This feature, to be released in the coming weeks, provides a quick overview of the materials consumed and resources used in the production of a product, along with their corresponding emissions

I can analyze the entire production value chain, from start to finish, to precisely assess the carbon footprint and pinpoint CO2 equivalent (CO2E) impacts at various stages, for example:

  • How much carbon emissions are caused by one supplier compared to another?
  • Which production line is the most energy-intensive? What kinds of improvements can be made across the line to enhance energy efficiency?

These are actionable insights which translate to process improvements:

  • Comparing carbon emissions among different suppliers to identify greener alternatives.
  • Identifying the most energy-intensive production lines and recommending improvements across the line to enhance energy efficiency.Once we have identified and set up our Opex/capex projects to improve the process, we can then closely monitor and track the energy consumption reductions. In addition, we can also share the footprint of our finished goods using SDX with our customers, just as we requested this information from our suppliers. 

We also offer comprehensive standard reports that provide an overview of all emissions within a company. These reports include:

  • A summary of the inflow and outflow of emissions for the specified period.
  • A detailed breakdown of emissions categorized according to the Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Protocol.
  • The top five products with the highest emissions, whether purchased, sold, or otherwise.
  • Identification of the highest energy consumers, including production lines, facilities, and process infrastructure.
  • A split of emissions by energy source, enabling quick tracking of the proportion of green electricity and green gas, particularly if specific targets have been set for these elements.

As a summary, we have addressed the following questions in this blog:

  1. How can we electronically request supplier footprint information?
  2. How can we incorporate this footprint into purchasing processes within the core ERP system for effective decision-making?
  3. How can we leverage actual production data—such as machine hours, setup time, energy consumption, and energy bills—to accurately model and calculate a carbon footprint at the product level, instead of relying on averages or cost-based data for your Environmental Product Declaration?
  4. How can we electronically share these calculated footprints of manufactured products with your customers?
  5. What kind of reports are available out of the box to accelerate carbon reporting ?

In the next blog, I am very excited to talk about our latest offering, “Green Ledger,” which unites finance and carbon accounting. I shall talk about how data from SFM gets integrated into Green ledger and the integration with Green ledger facilitates the conversion of these footprints into financial journal entries, enabling a holistic and integrated approach to sustainability.

entry.title: Simulation vs Runtime Discrepancy in B2B MAG-Compliant Mapping (DELVRY05 → EDI 856)
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I'm working on a B2B integration scenario using SAP Integration Suite with Integration Advisor, mapping IDoc DELVRY05 to EDI X12 856. The mapping is fully MAG-compliant, and I’m using XSLT logic to calculate and generate the DTM*017 segment.

Problem,

When I simulate the message in Integration Advisor, the DTM*017 segment is generated correctly.

However, when the same message is processed in a real B2B runtime scenario, the segment is not generated at all — even though the payload is identical in both cases.

Simulation Output, 

B2B runtime scenario,

Expected Output,

 

XSLT Logic Using,

<xsl:if test="($nodes_in/QUALF)[1] = '006'">
  <!-- grab the raw string -->
  <xsl:variable name="raw" select="normalize-space(($nodes_in/ISDD)[1])"/>
  <!-- convert to xs:date -->
  <xsl:variable name="date" 
        select="xs:date(concat(
          substring($raw,1,4), '-', 
          substring($raw,5,2), '-', 
          substring($raw,7,2)
        ))"/>
  <!-- add 3 days and format back to YYYYMMDD -->
  <xsl:value-of select="
    format-date(
      $date + xs:dayTimeDuration('P3D'),
      '[Y0001][M01][D01]'
    )
  "/>
</xsl:if>

✅Simulation Output (Working):

❌Runtime Output (Fails):

As you can see, the DTM*017 segment is missing at runtime, although the input data is the same.

 Request

Has anyone experienced this simulation/runtime mismatch in Integration Suite, Could you please help us to resolve this issue.

Thanks in advance for your support!
— Darshana

entry.title: TR import outside scheduled job in continual cycle
entry.content:
Hi Team,

 

In our ChaRM setup, we have defined an import strategy and scheduled background jobs for TR imports into the production environment.

 

Current Setup:

 

  • TR imports into production are scheduled via background job.
  • TR imports are completely restricted during weekends.

 

Requirement: We have a business need to import specific Transport Requests (TRs) into the production system in case of priority changes, under the following conditions: 

 

  • During weekends, or
  • Outside the scheduled import job time (e.g., in the evening).

 

However, the current task list configuration does not allow us to select and import individual change TRs into the respective target systems.

 

So Is there any standard configuration or recommended approach in ChaRM that allows importing a specific ChaRM TR into the production environment outside the scheduled import job, especially in a continual cycle?
also I have attached the snapshot of non-editable task-list for reference.

 

Please check and advise on how we can achieve this while remaining compliant with ChaRM best practices.

 

Thanks!


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Local Mocking of External Service not working
entry.content: The issue is resolved when rebuilding the local sqlite database via "cds deploy --to sqlite --with-mocks". This builds the csv data into the local sqlite file including the csvs located in srv/external/data and resolves the error above.

entry.title: Re: <ReqdExctnDt> has to pick Today + 1 day in payment file
entry.content:
Hello @Kavitha_141

Thanks for your message and details. The <ReqdExctnDt> tag in the payment file is based on the Value Date entered during the payment run in S4 Public cloud.

Settings in SSCUI 101045 or the “Days” field in the house bank only affect cash position planning, not the execution date in the file.

To get today + 1, please manually set the Value Date to the next day when starting the payment run


Here are some useful official SAP references

1.SAP Help – Manage Automatic Payments  Covers how the payment run parameters (including Value Date) influence payment processing and file generation. 

2. SAP KBA 2954462 – ReqdExctnDt Value in Payment File (SAP login required)
Explains how <ReqdExctnDt> in payment files is typically taken from Value Date and confirms this behaviour for standard SAP-delivered payment formats

Let me know if you need further support

With kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: Re: All leaf elements must be used as part of the associa...
entry.content:
Hi Martin,

thank you very much!

The only thing, that was missing, is the compounded key in the MD View, which I have associated with 2_GV_CS_ITEM_HIERWITHDIRECTORY_02. The error message was misleading..

“The flattening” was already correct. I do it in a little bit different way, I use calculated columns -> like described in the mentioned link

Hierarchien aus SAP BW in Datasphere abbilden: So geht's

It is need to be done for each InfoObject from the IOBJNM column of the BW hierarchy table (/BI0/H*, in my case /BI0/HCS_ITEM).

In normal case these are 0HIER_NODE and the InfoObject itself.

KNOTEN: case when IOBJNM = '0HIER_NODE' then NODENAME else null end

UNTERPOSITION: case when IOBJNM = '0CS_ITEM' then NODENAME else null end

CS_CHAR, CS_ITEM and NODE_WO_COMPND (WO_COMPND = without compounding) are created with "String" functions

CS_ITEM: RIGHT("UNTERPOSITION", 10) CS_CHART: LEFT(NODENAME, 2)

NODE_WO_COMPND: RIGHT("KNOTEN", (LENGTH("KNOTEN") - 2))

Best regards

Nadine

entry.title: Re: Add System Information on Fiori Launchpad
entry.content:
Yes. Available for SAP S/4HANA since release 2021. Use the Launchpad Configuration Parameters in transaction /UI2/FLP_CUS_CONF to set the System Information bar on. All the parameters start with SYS_INFO_...

Very nice! Works a treat! 

Also see the guide...

https://help.sap.com/docs/ABAP_PLATFORM_NEW/a7b390faab1140c087b8926571e942b7/eae3cc31b1d34d139d153067fcecd975.html?

entry.content:
I am reaching out to seek assistance regarding the configuration of the Fiori URL as OAuth2. I have successfully created a profile through Tcode OA2C_CONFIG and have integrated OKTA as the Identity Provider (IDP). Additionally, I can obtain an Access token via Tcode OA2C_GRANT, and the flow is properly directing to the Fiori URL.

However, I am encountering an issue with the Fiori link that appears following the redirection. When I open the URL in a new private browser session, it initially directs me to the OKTA login details, which is expected. Yet, after redirecting to the Fiori URL, it prompts for SAP credentials. Ideally, the authentication should solely occur via the OKTA token without requiring any SAP credentials.

I would like to bring to your attention that I have already set up the email in the user alias through SU01, which I am using in OKTA.

Additionally, I have experimented with different logon methods for Fiori through SICF. When I selected Basic authentication, it still requests SAP credentials, and for other logon methods, I am receiving an unauthorized error.

Could you please provide guidance or support on resolving this issue? I appreciate your assistance in advance.
